text: The patient is positioned in the low-lithotomy position, which involves lying on their back with legs elevated and supported in stirrups.
The surgeon positions both of the patient's legs in Allen stirrups, maintaining the low-lithotomy position for the procedure.
The surgeon checks the patient's heels in the Allen stirrups to ensure there is no undue tension or pressure on the posterior compartment of the legs.
The surgeon adjusts and secures the stirrups in place after ensuring the patient's heels are properly positioned.
The arms are wrapped and placed in generous foam padding, similar to preparation for a laparoscopic case.
The tuck sheet is wrapped over the patient's arm and secured under the mattress.
The patient's hands are positioned neutrally, ensuring they remain relaxed and aligned with the body.
The image shows an arm positioned outside of the neutral alignment, contrasting with the intended setup.
The surgeon positions the arm in a neutral position, ensuring foam padding is placed within the hand for support.
The tuck sheet is positioned from underneath the patient, over the foam on the arm, and tucked beneath the mattress, securing the arm in place.
Foam or a sheet is placed over the patient's chest at the level of the nipples, and the patient is taped to the bed snugly, ensuring the tape is not too tight.
The surgeon demonstrates the placement of foam over the patient's chest and arms, ensuring there is enough space to fit a few fingers between the foam and the body for proper positioning.
The Foley catheter is positioned over the patient's right leg, visible in the current setup.
The patient's upper extremities are visibly padded and securely positioned with foam and tuck sheets, ensuring stability and protection during the procedure.
A thick foam pad is placed over the patient, spanning from the neck down to the buttocks, providing cushioning and support.
The surgeon tests the securement of the patient to the bed by adjusting it to the maximum Trendelenburg position, ensuring there is no slippage.
The image shows the SI robot docked, with its arms positioned around the surgical bed.
The diagram shows the number two and three robotic arms broken in the opposite direction using large joints, ensuring adequate distance between all robotic arms.
